data.title: CECO Environmental: Impressive Business Momentum, Too Much Share Momentum
data.description:
In May, I concluded that the market was awaiting margin improvements in the case of CECO Environmental (CECO). The business saw strong sales growth, yet margins remained volatile, as that latter observation made me a bit cautious on the shares.

That conclusion has been far too conservative with the benefit of hindsight, as shares have doubled in the half a year ever since, now trading around the $52 mark. While orders and sales are growing spectacularly and margins are improving, I just cannot escape the feeling that the move has been overextended.

A Leading and Global Industrial Environmental Solutions Business

The paragraph header is how CECO describes its own business, which was founded in 1966. The company has a mission to protect people, the environment, and industrial equipment.

The company focuses 100% on industrial clients, these including general industrial companies, beverage can producers, food & beverage producers, waste and wastewater providers, semiconductors, renewable technology providers, data center operators, natural gas infrastructure providers, among others.

Energy transition products make up about 40% of sales, these including emission management, thermal & acoustic management, combustion management, etc. Industrial air makes up 35% of sales, with solutions and products provided including silencers, scrubbers, dust removals, etc. Industrial water makes up the remaining quarter of sales, this including supply water and wastewater solutions, and others.

While the business has been active in these markets for many decades, the performance in the past was rather uneven, to put it mildly. After all, a $15 stock in 2013 traded at just $5 in 2022, but ever since was unleashed following a period of a real strategic transition, complemented by targeted M&A efforts.

A $350 million revenue base in 2015 rose to just over $400 million in 2022, these revenues accompanied by single-digit operating margins, with modest dilution incurred over this period of time.

Momentum Accelerates

The momentum for CECO arrived in 2022, a year in which revenues rose by 30% to $422 million, while orders rose some 46% to $526 million, with adjusted earnings tripling to $0.76 per share.

While the strategic actions looked to be a solid explanation for the momentum observed, the strength felt a bit unexpected and surprising, as sales rose by another 29% to $545 million in 2023. During 2024, the company announced a $125 million acquisition of Profire Energy, with this company adding $60 million in annual sales. Moreover, the company announced the purchase of Verantis as well, adding another $45 million in sales.

Both these deals only closed early in 2025, making that 2024 revenues were up just 2% to $558 million, as the backlog rose another 14% to $667 million. Adjusted EBITDA was up 9% to $63 million, with adjusted earnings flattish at $0.75 per share, these earnings being quite adjusted.

Many Moving Targets

Following the release of the 2024 results, the company guided for 2025 sales to move up to $700-$750 million, with EBITDA seen at $90-$100 million, aided by M&A moves, as the company announced the divestment of its fluid handling business in April. This deal was valued at $110 million.

First quarter sales rose by 40% to $177 million, as the order intake was reported at $228 million, up 57% on the year, adding to a backlog that exceeds $600 million. While that was really impressive, adjusted operating income was down 16% to just $8.6 million, with adjusted earnings down a penny to $0.10 per share.

However, these softer earnings were achieved on the back of a $14 million adjusted EBITDA number, which annualised came in far short compared to the $90-$100 million guidance for the year. With 37 million shares trading at $25 and net debt seen just shy of $200 million, the enterprise valuation of $1.1 billion looked very reasonable in relation to a $700 million sales guidance, equal to around 1.5 times sales.

However, the issue was that of margins, as I only saw full-year adjusted earnings around a dollar per share based on the guidance, with real volatility expected around tariffs, which made it hard to build conviction around the $25 mark.

On Fire

Other than the regular quarterly results, it has been rather quiet on the corporate front this year. Second quarter sales rose by 35% to $185 million, as a 95% increase in the order intake to $274 million was outright spectacular. These advancements resulted in some operating leverage, with adjusted EBITDA reported at $23 million and change, with adjusted earnings reported at $0.24 per share.

Following the sales momentum, the full-year sales guidance was hiked by twenty-five million to $725-$775 million, while the EBITDA guidance was maintained.

Third-quarter results were released by the end of October. Revenues rose by 46% to $198 million, with orders up 44% to $233 million, adding to a $720 million backlog. EBITDA was reported at $23 million, equal to the second quarter, with adjusted earnings reported at $0.26 per share; these numbers look quite clean.

The company maintained the guidance on all fronts for 2025, as the company offered an upbeat guidance for 2026. Revenues are seen at $850-$950 million next year, with EBITDA seen up to $110-$130 million. This suggests that some real momentum could be seen as earnings trend around a dollar per share now.

The outlook calls for a $30 million incremental EBITDA contribution, which, given a low depreciation intensity of the business, could make that pre-tax profits per share could jump by about eighty cents based on a share tally of 36 million shares. This could create a realistic roadmap for earnings to move up to $1.50 per share next year. In either case, expectations have risen on this front to a mid-thirty times earnings multiple based on such projections for 2026 earnings.

By now, the 36 million shares are valued at just over $1.8 billion, for a $2.0 billion enterprise valuation based on a stable net debt load of $188 million. This values the business at around 2-2.5 times sales, as EBITDA multiples are moving up to the high teens, with both sales and margins moving in the right direction.

And Now?

Part of the re-rating is certainly deserved, as momentum is simply very strong after lots of self-help, which is to be applauded. However, real earnings power is still lagging a bit, as further margin gains could provide more support to the investment case here.

What is clear is that CECO is no longer the same business as it was in the past, aided by strong order gains and real growth, although the heavy work now has to come from further margin gains. I have no concerns on the topline, certainly not as 2026 orders are seen topping a billion.

While EBITDA margins are seen up nearly a point to 13% and change in 2026, I have taken note that free cash flow conversion from this EBITDA number is seen only at 50-60% next year, compared to a 60% target this year.

Given all this, I congratulate the business a lot but have concerns about the realistic margins, which would not be an issue if not for the fact that shares doubled so far this year. This makes me naturally a bit cautious here, and while I applaud the achievements of the business so far, I fail to see a compelling risk-reward here.

data.title: Experian: Attractive Setup Given Cheap Relative Valuation And Solid Fundamentals
data.description:
Investment overview

I give a buy rating for Experian (OTCQX:EXPGF). The Ascend platform is well embedded in the workflow of major lenders, while the Consumer Services business has scaled into a platform with real data advantages. Add to that consistent organic growth, expanding margins, and a relative valuation sitting near multi-year lows, and I see a setup where the risk-reward looks very attractive.

Business description

EXPGF is a data and software business that helps clients manage risk and fraud and make better decisions. There are five main verticals: financial services, health, automotive, insurance, and marketing, each with its own large proprietary datasets. EXPGF also has a direct-to-consumer [consumer services] arm that helps consumers to track credit, protect identities, and handle day-to-day financial matters.

1H26 earnings

The 1H26 headline numbers were strong, with organic revenue growing by 8% y/y, landing at the top of guidance, and the sequential trend moving the right way (Q1 came in at 8% y/y, and Q2 accelerated to 9% y/y). On a constant currency basis [CCB], total revenue grew even stronger at 12% y/y. By region, North America remains the main growth driver. Organic growth saw 10% y/y in 1H, with B2B up 12% y/y and consumer sub-segment organic growth up 8% y/y. The profit performance was also very strong. Benchmark EBIT (this is EXPGF's non-GAAP measure) grew 14% y/y, with margins moving up as well, expanding 50 bps CCB and 30 bps on a reported basis. The more important metric was the 100 bps organic margin expansion, which shows us that EXPGF is benefiting from meaningful operating leverage, despite EXPGF integrating new acquisitions that are temporarily margin dilutive. This translated to a strong benchmark EPS growth of 12% y/y.

The Ascend Platform

I believe the Ascend platform has a very strong edge. This is not just any typical analytics toolkit; I see it as a core infrastructure for the financial institutions in the US and UK. To put things into perspective (per the RBC 2025 TIMT conference), more than 20 of the top 25 US financial institutions now use an Ascend module, and in the UK, about two-thirds of all lending volume depends on Ascend in some form. Clearly, this Ascend platform is part of the workflow, and given this level of integration, it becomes incredibly hard for a competitor to displace since ripping out Ascend would disrupt a significant amount of lending (too much operational risk).

Here is how I see the competitive landscape. Some competitors might offer a very strong point solution, like a single decisioning module, or sell data feeds, and these could be better than EXPGF solutions on a stand-alone basis. But they cannot compare to EXPGF solutions as a whole, one that combines data, fraud-prevention tools, etc., in one ecosystem. Take the case of a bank: Ascend becomes the operating system that helps it make credit decisions. Throughout the entire lifecycle, from building credit models to deployment and monitoring risk, the switching costs become massive.

Consumer Services

Similar to the Ascend platform, I believe EXPGF's Consumer Services business has reached a scale where it looks less like an optional credit-monitoring tool and more like a core financial platform for consumers. To give a sense of scale, there are now more than 208 million members globally, and this is a massive distribution footprint, which I expect to only get larger. The most important driver for this is the well-executed connected-data strategy. In North America, EXPGF now has 18 million connected bank accounts, and this is a huge edge vs. peers that do not have this function. Banks that are connected provide real-time visibility into income, spending patterns, and bill payments, which are important proprietary data points that a credit report doesn't provide.

As you can imagine, with more sophisticated data, EXPGF can run analytics to identify consumers with high purchase intent (for various opportunities) with precision. One good case study is the insurance marketplace. Because EXPGF can see both the consumer's current insurance provider and their renewal timing, it can easily push through a personalized quote that fits the user's needs (at the right timing). And this timing advantage is something a stand-alone competitor cannot replicate without similar data access.

Layered on top of that is the flywheel effect, which accelerates the path of scaling up. The flywheel impact is quite obvious. As a consumer gets a better offer or a more convenient experience, the corresponding partners also get higher-quality leads, and if you see this as a marketplace model, with more demand comes more supply, which attracts more demand. Underlying all these is a larger "demand/supply" liquidity pool, which opens the path for EXPNGF to attract partners from other verticals like automotive and other financial management tools.

The Data Moat

The main bear case argument for EXPGF is that AI will somehow weaken its data advantage. I disagree completely on this. My view is that AI models don't replace the value of EXPGF data; they magnify EXPGF data moat. Here is food for thought. EXPGF has one of the best datasets in the market; as mentioned above, in North America alone, it has >12,000 contributors feeding it with information, and it ingests ~1.1 billion new data points every month (source: RBC conference). That is a lot of data, and it is not something one can just use AI to scrape from the open web. This is regulated credit information that is private and collected through formal agreements.

Let me take a step further to explain why AI is not going to displace the EXPGF data moat. The important point here is that the moat isn't just the breadth of data but the underlying infrastructure. You simply cannot recreate the credit history of millions of Americans by throwing AI at the problem (where is AI going to get all the data anyway?). You need thousands of bank relationships, regulatory approvals, and a scalable working system to manage all this sensitive data at scale. At the end of the day, the quality of the AI model depends on the inputs, and the EXPGF edge here is significant. Hence, I believe instead of being bypassed by AI, the EXPGF moat has gotten a lot stronger since it can use AI to further widen the gap between itself and the next sub-scale competitor.

Valuation

Despite delivering consistent organic revenue and adj. EPS growth, EXPGF stock has de-rated significantly to levels that I find too cheap. It now trades at 23x forward PE, which is the lowest point in recent history relative to the Stoxx 600 index. I don't think there is anything fundamentally wrong with EXPGF, and hence, this relative valuation seems like a compelling entry point. My best guess is that the market is reacting to fears about AI disruption, but as I have explained above, there is nothing to worry about. If anything, AI amplifies EXPGF's moat.

For a business with products that are becoming a core utility for businesses and consumers, with consistent growth and expanding margins, paying a multi-year low relative multiple for a business doesn't seem aggressive. Once the market realizes that there is nothing to worry about with AI disruption, we should see investor confidence come back online, and coupled with continuous earnings growth, I expect a re-rating in the coming quarters. If the relative multiple goes back to the average of ~2x, this implies EXPGF forward PE to be ~29x, or 26% upside just based on multiples re-rating.

Risk

A key risk to look out for is regulatory friction. The recent CFPB lawsuit regarding consumer dispute processes is a reminder that regulators are still scrutinizing this industry. Any adverse change here could materially disrupt how EXPGF operates. FX swings are also going to continue weighing on reported results, which may trigger sell-offs on the stock as it masks the underlying momentum.

Conclusion

I recommend a buy rating for EXPGF. The competitive advantage is strong, with both Ascend and Consumer Services moving in the right direction to become core infrastructure tools rather than just simple add-ons. The market's fear of AI disruption is incorrect, in my view, as it actually strengthens EXPGF's edge. With continuous earnings growth, improving margins, and a valuation well below historical norms, I believe now is a good entry point.

data.title: Virtus KAR International Small-Mid Cap Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

The MSCI All Country World Ex USA Small Mid Cap Index advanced 6.22% during the third quarter, pushing the year-to-date return to 26.78%. Speculation around artificial intelligence (AI) has been a seemingly all-pervasive market focus in recent months. Sectors perceived to benefit from AI, like materials and information technology, performed strongly, while more defensive sectors, like consumer staples and communication services, underperformed. As a potential sign of the level of speculation in the market, unprofitable companies collectively more than doubled the return of the benchmark during the quarter.

Fund Performance

The Virtus KAR International Small-Mid Cap Fund returned -4.98% (Class I) in the quarter, underperforming the MSCI All Country World Ex USA Small Mid Cap Index. Given our focus on profitable companies with established competitive positions, the outperformance of unprofitable companies in areas like AI where the competitive landscape is shifting rapidly has been a strong headwind to performance.

Haw Par and Caixa Seguridade Participacoes were the largest contributors to performance during the quarter.

  • Haw Par (OTCPK:HAWPF)(OTCPK:HAWPY) is an investment holding company listed in Singapore. The company reported solid business results and benefited from overall outperformance of Singapore equities during the quarter.
  • Caixa Seguridade Participacoes is one of the largest insurance brokers in Brazil. As we see it, the company has attractive joint venture interests in the entities that underwrite the policies Caixa distributes. Shares closed ahead in the quarter, with strong quarter-end share price performance driven by continued strength in mortgage insurance despite elevated and rising rates. Parts of Caixa Seguridade’s business remain challenged by macro headwinds, but we believe the long-term underlying business drivers that initially attracted us to the investment case remain intact.

Baltic Classifieds and Sopra Steria Group were the largest detractors from performance in the quarter.

  • Listed in London, Baltic Classifieds operates several online classified portals across the Baltic states of Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia. Full-year guidance was below expectations, driven by weakness in the Estonian auto market because of a new auto tax.
  • Sopra Steria (OTCPK:SPSAF) is one of the largest IT services providers in Europe. The company partners with governments, financial services companies, and aerospace and defense companies to provide technology deployments and support. The company reported weak results as the difficult demand environment weighed on Sopra’s business.

data.title: TCW Core Fixed Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Now Showing: A Cooling Labor Market

If a swift and sharp recovery from tariff-driven volatility was the surprise Spring blockbuster, then a weakening labor market – evidenced by underwhelming payroll prints and significant downward revisions – was the summer flop. Combined with other employment indicators pointing to a low-hiring, low-firing state of the job market, the possibility of a further deterioration prompted the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) to implement a “risk-management cut” with a 25 basis point (BP) reduction to the policy rate. While swaps pricing reflected a unanimous vote by market participants for the rate cut by the time the September meeting arrived, the tenor from within the FOMC over the quarter appeared to be anything but. Indeed, two Governors dissented at the July meeting (the first two-Governor dissent in decades) in favor of an earlier start to rate cuts, while others cautioned against easing in the face of still-elevated inflation. Despite the divide in opinions, preventing any further softening in the labor market was shown to be top priority with the realized rate cut and median FOMC projection of two more cuts by year end. However, there remains lingering uncertainty among some market participants about potential longer-term tariff impacts and the Federal Reserve’s (the Fed) independence that weighed on longer yields, resulting in their relative underperformance versus the front of the curve. Illustratively, 1- and 2-Year yields fell by 35 and 11 bps, respectively, while the 10- and 30-Year yields moved lower by 8 and 4 bps, respectively.

Despite the potentially troublesome developments that forced the Fed’s hand, risk assets turned in another quarter of strong returns as investors appeared to focus more on the valuation impacts of lower rates and less on the underlying reason for the rate cut. Equities once again outperformed with the S&P 500 Index gaining 8% while the tech-heavy NASDAQ Index returned more than 11%. Though more modest, fixed income markets also generated positive returns, with the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index finishing the quarter up 2%. Excluding the rate impact on bonds, the excess return (outperformance versus similar duration Treasuries) for the Aggregate Bond Index reached nearly 50 bps as yield spreads tightened across sectors. The strongest return profile belonged to corporate credit with investment grade and high yield issues gaining 2.6% and 2.5%, respectively, supported by robust demand. With the future path of rates coming into greater clarity over the period, investors sought to lock in corporate yields at still-attractive levels, which also prompted a wave of record supply in both investment grade and high yield markets as borrowers in turn sought to capitalize on receptive capital markets and favorable borrowing costs (falling yields, reduced spreads). Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) gained 2.4% alongside declining interest rate volatility to lead securitized products, while the non-agency residential MBS sector saw broad-based advances across collateral types as spreads moved tighter and prices increased. Commercial MBS (CMBS) gained 1.8%, with positive total returns from both agency and private label deals, though private label issues outperformed given the risk-on backdrop. Finally, Index-eligible asset-backed securities (ABS) gained 1.6%, though auto and credit card collateral underperformed non-traditional sectors like collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) which benefitted from the strong appetite for spread and yield premiums offered, especially relative to corporates of similar quality and maturity.

Performance

The TCW Core Fixed Income Fund I-Class (“Fund”) gained 1.98% net of fees in the third quarter of 2025 to finish five bps behind the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (“Index”), while year-to-date performance remained 22 bps ahead of the Index. For the quarter, modest underperformance was driven by the Fund’s continued underweight to investment grade corporate credit as corporate spreads continued to tightened. Meanwhile, the Fund benefitted from the broad overweight to securitized sectors, particularly residential MBS. Agency MBS rewarded relative returns given the strong performance at the sector level amid easing rate volatility, while the Fund’s emphasis on lower and belly coupons within the 30-Year coupon stack provided an additional boost from an issue selection standpoint. Though this coupon positioning was a drag early in the year when the “carry trade” dominated and premium coupons benefitted, it has shifted to a tailwind that now supports year-to-date relative outperformance amid heightened prepayment concerns that have negatively impacted premium coupon performance in favor of the total return potential of discount-priced coupons. Away from residential MBS, the overweight position in private label CMBS also contributed, with a small additional boost from the focus on single asset single borrower deal structures which have performed well year-to-date in the broader risk-on backdrop and continued sponsorship of performing properties. Finally, the duration position was trimmed alongside the decline in Treasury yields during the quarter, but remained longer-than-Index, producing a modest benefit to relative performance.

Outlook & Positioning

We entered the year with a recognition that the distribution of possible economic outcomes had become wider given the administration's penchant for volatility, with a bias towards more pessimistic possibilities. While the view remains that the economy is likely to slow from here, it has become increasingly clear that policy sequencing matters, and that the administration's more aggressive policies and attendant headwinds appear to be fading. In contrast, there are components of the administration's agenda that are pro-growth and that are likely to provide economic tailwinds in the future, but the ability of the economy to bridge the gap between then and now is made more complicated by the demonstrable weakening that has occurred in the labor market. This development in our view is the single-largest determinant for the future direction of the economy; the jobs market turning from “low-hiring, low-firing” to “no-hiring and firing” likely means the difference between a mere slowing of growth and something that more closely resembles a recession, while the speed with which the pro-growth policy can be implemented and provide economic support is also set to play a meaningful yet protracted part. Regardless of which path the economy ultimately travels, pricing across most risk assets appears to be focused on only the most optimistic of outcomes as equity markets continue to reach new highs and credit spreads remain near historical lows, providing investors minimal protection against future volatility and necessitating diligent underwriting.

As value investors, TCW remains vigilant in its assessment of risks and opportunities across markets. With current corporate spread levels presenting an asymmetric risk and reward profile, the position in corporate credit continues to represent a sizeable underweight versus the Index, with selective exposure that is more defensive in nature (up in quality, focus on non-cyclical industries, emphasis on liquidity). Securitized products, however, currently offer a more compelling relative value argument and thus represent a more attractive segment of the market in which to allocate portfolio risk budgets. Agency MBS represents the largest position given the sector's liquidity, spread premiums, and government guarantee, with exposure focused on lower and belly coupons for pull-to-par price potential and better call protection, respectively. Non-agency MBS is a large off-Index allocation, favored for robust collateral profiles across that allow for various opportunities to capture strong risk-adjusted yields. Meanwhile, CMBS exposure is focused on single asset single borrower deals, selected based on a detailed analysis of the underlying property and sponsor that facilitates bottom-up inclusion criteria. ABS positions similarly reflect targeted positions including senior CLO tranches and high-quality non-traditional collateral types. Finally, expectations for rates to continue moving lower and for the Fed to move more quickly than anticipated by markets should the labor market weaken further informs a still longer-than-index duration position of approximately 0.2 years, and an overweight to the front-end of the curve.

GLOSSARY OF TERMS

Agency MBS – The purchase of mortgage-backed securities issued by government-sponsored enterprises such as Ginnie Mae, Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Asset-Backed Securities – A financial security backed by a loan, lease or receivables against assets other than real estate and mortgage-backed securities. Basis Point (BPS) – One hundredth of one percent, used chiefly in expressing differences of interest rates. Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index or “the Agg” – Is a broad-based fixed-income index used by bond traders and the managers of mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFS) as a benchmark to measure their relative performance. Bond – A fixed income instrument that represents a loan made by an investor to a borrower (typically corporate or governmental). A bond could be thought of as an I.O.U. between the lender and borrower that includes the details of the loan and its payments. A bond has an end date when the principal of the loan is due to be paid to the bond owner and usually includes the terms for variable or fixed interest payments that will be made by the borrower. Bonds are used by companies, municipalities, states, and sovereign governments to finance projects and operations. Owners of bonds are debtholders, or creditors, of the issuer. Call – To force an option writer to sell shares of stock at a price stipulated in a contract. Carry Trade – A strategy in which an investor sells a certain currency with a relatively low interest rate and uses the funds to purchase a different currency yielding a higher interest rate. CMBS (Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities) – A debt obligation that represents claims to the cash flows from pools of mortgage loans on commercial property. CLO (Collateralized Loan Obligations) – A special purpose vehicle with securitization payments in the form of different tranches. Financial institutions back this security with receivables from loans. Collateral – Property or other assets that a borrower offers a lender to secure a loan. Corporate – Of or relating to a bond issued by a corporation as opposed to a bond issued by the U.S. Treasury, a non-U.S. government or a municipality. Corporate Credit – A term that is used in written investment materials and commentaries to refer to a corporation's debt or to the corporate debt market as a whole. Coupon – The interest rate stated on a bond when it's issued. The coupon is typically paid semiannually. Duration – A measure of the sensitivity of the price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. Duration is expressed as a number of years. Rising interest rates mean falling bond prices, while declining interest rates mean rising bond prices. Excess Return – The rate of return on a security or an investment portfolio in excess of the return generated by a portfolio of U.S. Treasury securities with similar duration and partial durations. Federal Reserve (the Fed) – The central bank of the United States which regulates the U.S. monetary and financial system. FOMC (Federal Open Market Committee) – The branch of the Federal Reserve Board that determines the direction of monetary policy. Headwinds – Headwinds in an economic situation represent events or conditions e.g. a credit crisis, rising costs, natural disasters, etc, that slow down the growth of an economy. Inflation – A condition of a rise in the general level of prices of goods and services in an economy over a period of time. Investment Grade – A bond that is rated Baa3/BBB- or higher by Moody's, Standard & Poors and Fitch. Labor Market – The nominal market in which workers find paying work, employers find willing workers, and wage rates are determined. Liquidity – The ability to convert an asset to cash quickly. Nasdaq – Nasdaq is a global electronic marketplace for buying and selling securities, as well as the benchmark index for U.S. technology stocks. Nasdaq was created by the National Association of Securities Dealers (NASD) to enable investors to trade securities on a computerized, speedy and transparent system, and commenced operations on February 8, 1971. The term “Nasdaq” is also used to refer to the Nasdaq Composite, an index of more than 3,000 stocks listed on the Nasdaq exchange that includes the world's foremost technology and biotech giants such as Apple, Google, Microsoft, Oracle, Amazon, Intel and Amgen. Non-agency – Mortgage backed securities made up of mortgage loans that are not guaranteed by a government-supported agency. Non-Agency MBS – Mortgage backed securities sponsored by private companies other than government sponsored enterprises such as Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac. Non-cyclical – Non-cyclical stocks repeatedly outperform the market when economic growth slows. Non-cyclical securities are generally profitable regardless of economic trends because they produce or distribute goods and services we always need, including things like food, power, water, and gas. Overweight – A condition where the portfolio exposure to a given asset class (or risk measure) exceeds that of the benchmark index. Recession – Two consecutive quarters of negative economic growth as measured by a country's gross domestic product. Risk Asset – Any asset that carries a degree of risk. S&P 500 – The S&P 500 Index, or Standard & Poor's 500 Index, is a market-capitalization-weighted index of 500 leading publicly traded companies in the U.S. The index actually has 503 components because three of them have two share classes listed. Securitized – When a bank pools together different kinds of loans to clear them off its balance sheet, to free up credit for new lenders, and to create new securities that can be marketed and sold to investors. Spread – The difference between the bid and the ask price of a security or asset. Swaps – The most common and simplest swap, an agreement between two parties to exchange sequences of cash flows for a set period of time. Tariff – A tax imposed on imported goods and services. Total Return – The rate of return on a security, including income from dividends and interest, as well as appreciation or depreciation in the price of the security, over a given time period of time. Tranches – Are segments created from a pool of securities, usually debt instruments such as bonds or mortgages, that are divided up by risk, time to maturity, or other characteristics in order to be marketable to different investors. Each portion or tranche of a securitized or structured product is one of several related securities offered at the same time, but with varying risks, rewards and maturities to appeal to a diverse range of investors. Underperform – When an investment is

underperforming, it is not keeping pace with other securities or indices. Underweight – A condition where a portfolio does not hold a sufficient amount of a particular security when compared to the security's weight in the underlying benchmark portfolio. Valuations – The process of determining the current worth of an asset or company. There are many techniques that can be used to determine value, some are subjective and others are objective. Volatility – A measure of the risk of price moves for a security calculated from the standard deviation of day to day logarithmic historical price changes. Yield – The income return on an investment. This refers to the interest or dividends received from a security and is usually expressed annually as a percentage based on the investment's cost, its current market value or its face value. Yield Premium – Additional return.

data.title: KinderCare: Recovery Timeline Likely Pushed Back To A Later Period
data.description:
Summary

I gave a hold rating to KinderCare (KLC) in September this year, with my main concern being that the near-term headwinds still remain. Specifically, I believed the poor enrollment trends and occupancy performance overshadowed the improved visibility from the bipartisan funding support. The 3Q25 results didn't change my view (still hold rated). Enrollment and occupancy remain soft, and state-level execution on subsidies is evidently a risk that I failed to appreciate previously.

3Q 2025 Earnings Review

KLC 3Q25 numbers continued to paint a mixed picture. Revenue came in at $676.8 million, up 0.8% y/y, but the main Early Childhood Education [ECE] business continues to be flat at $627 million. While pricing was up ~2%, it was offset by a similar drop in enrollment. It was nice to see that the Champions segment continued to print solid growth of 10.7% y/y, driving the revenue base to ~$50 million, but it is just not enough to offset softness in the core segment. You don't see the real damage from this lackluster core performance at the top line, but you do see it in the profit margins. While overall topline was up slightly y/y, adj. EBITDA fell 7% y/y to $66.4 million, with margins sliding to <10%. This is largely due to the fixed-cost model of the ECE segment. When enrollment dips, the cost base stays the same, and coupled with SG&A moving higher by 109 bps due to the push for tech investments, it directly impacts profitability. Some may celebrate that EPS jumped to $0.13 from $0.05, but this came almost entirely from ~$15 million lower interest expense after the IPO refinancing.

Positive Developments

The good news for shareholders is there are real signs that the longer-term story is still intact, especially around the efforts to diversify the business. As highlighted above, the Champions segment grew revenue by 10.7% y/y, which was driven by 120 net new sites added over the past year. Clearly, KLC has a scalable model in place, which is very bullish, as there is still plenty of room to run, given KLC still reaches only a small slice of US elementary schools.

Champions as we sit here coming out of back-to-school is about 1,100 locations. We just added about 200 new ones, net this year, we're going to add about 120 plus or minus net to the count, which is great. There's -- depending on which one you look at, 60,000 to 90,000 elementary schools, we're currently the biggest private provider out there. The YMCA also does it. And so they're bigger than us. There's a lot of runway out there. Q3 2025 earnings call (by CFO)

The data from the B2B employer channel also reads bullish, with KLC signing 20 new employer contracts in Q3, pushing its coverage to 317,000 more employees. The key takeaway here, I believe, is that employer on-site centers have occupancy >70%, which is way ahead of community-based centers. And this is important because it proves that B2B has stickier demand, which means a more predictable revenue pool (which also supports what I said previously, as quoted below).

Relative to B2C, I believe the B2B segment is more important because employer contracts commit blocks of seats to KLC, and this gives visibility to forward occupancy, cuts marketing costs, improves payment reliability, and lengthens customer tenure. All these dynamics expand margins and make KLC's earnings stream resilient.

Another very important positive point to the equity story is development on the "opportunity region." We now have hard data to gain confidence that this strategy is working. Management noted that this cohort of weaker centers actually lifted occupancy by ~200 bps y/y (as a reminder, overall occupancy fell). This matters for the bull case because it shows occupancy weakness may not be a structural issue. It is something that can be fixed with the right execution. The prime example here is the Michigan turnaround. Occupancy went from 48% to 95% after KLC implemented the right execution (i.e., applying the new diagnostic playbook). Simply put, what this means is KLC relied on data to identify specific frictions in the sales funnel (e.g., poor engagement) and deploy solutions like "Family Builder" (a CRM tool) and online scheduling to ensure faster and more personalized interactions with families. If KLC can scale this approach across the weaker footprints it now has, the impact could be very meaningful (think about the operating leverage and impact to adj. EBITDA).

Negative Developments

However, I am sticking with my view that the KLC equity story is still not clear for a buy rating yet. I acknowledge the bright spots in B2B and the potential turnaround in occupancy rates, but the main concerns I flagged in my last write-up have actually worsened. Same-center occupancy fell 160 bps y/y to 67%, and full-time weekly enrollments were down by 190 bps. Management now expects full-year occupancy to finish ~200 bps below 2024 levels, which is a clear sign that the back-to-school season (note that this is usually the biggest driver of volume) didn't come through. What this means is that KLC will enter 2026 with a low base, and since rebuilding occupancy takes time (overall occupancy still fell despite the good efforts in Michigan), this lower occupancy base also means KLC will face a tough comp as it goes through 2026. If I am right, the weak headline numbers should continue to weigh on the stock sentiment.

I have also underestimated the risk in the "de-risked" funding story I noted before. To be clear, federal funding is stable, but the problem is at the state level. Indiana is the best example here. Per the Q3 transcript, the reconciliation of Indiana budget cut reimbursement rates and reduced authorized spots, in total, cost KLC ~1,000 enrollments. The knock-on effect is on two levels: (1) volume in the subsidy channel goes down, and (2) pricing comes in lighter (2% this year vs. the 3-5% expectation) because subsidy tuition is lower than private pay (i.e., negative mix shift impact). If more such cases happen (across other states), this entire bullish aspect of the equity story will quickly come under scrutiny by the market, as it means KLC's path for margin recovery will get even murkier.

Finally, the macro setup does not offer much help either. If anything, it remains very uncertain, as management pointed to a more cautious consumer backdrop, which has caused families to delay decisions, and they expect this to continue into 2026. Combined with the two negative points above, I simply don't see a clear path to a topline and profit recovery in 2026. Realistically, the recovery timeline is likely to have been pushed into 2027, and from now till then, I would think the stock will continue to stay in a "show me" territory.

Valuation

I believe the stock trades at 4.5x forward EBITDA (all-time low) for a good reason. Like I have said, the recovery timeline is extremely uncertain and is only likely to happen in 2027. Which means there are few reasons for investors to buy today. In the worst-case scenario, KLC could see a further dip in occupancy due to poor state-level execution and a worsening macro environment. I don't think investors are going to take the risk of investing in KLC given these conditions. I remain with the view that one should stay neutral on KLC until a better demand backdrop appears.

Conclusion

Bottom line, my view on KLC stays neutral. There are real positives in B2B and Champions, and the early signs from the "opportunity region" plan show the turnaround playbook works when execution is tight. But the core enrollment trend is still moving the wrong way, state-level subsidy execution also adds another layer of risk, and management now expects a cautious consumer backdrop to stretch into 2026. Until visibility improves, I think the stock remains firmly in "show me" territory.

data.title: Q3 2025 Insights From Gabelli CEF Growth Portfolio Managers
data.description:
The direction of travel for many things in the third quarter was “onward and upward.” Nowhere was this clearer than in markets, where the S&P 500 closed at all-time highs on 35% of all trading days, the most in a quarter since 1998. In entertainment, Netflix (NFLX)’s global dominance advanced further with the Korean animated film KPop Demon Hunters , which became the service’s most-watched film ever with ~325mm views. In geopolitics, the Trump Administration made progress with six more trade deals, highlighted by new frameworks with the European Union and Japan. In sports, Irishman Shane Lowry overcame a raucous New York crowd by holing a dramatic six-foot birdie putt to clinch the first European Ryder Cup win on U.S. soil since 2012.

The Economy

In contrast to such decisive direction of the markets during the third quarter, U.S. economic data was mixed. On one hand, softer labor market fundamentals and ongoing weakness in the manufacturing sector painted a picture of an economy going through a soft patch. On the other, various “soft data” indicators suggested sentiment is improving across both business and consumer sectors, and prospects for easier monetary policy could set up for accelerating growth trends ahead.

Beginning with the labor market, the Bureau of Labor Statistics’ widely-followed Establishment Survey showed negative nonfarm payrolls growth in aggregate during June for the first time since 2020. This reading surfaced on a delayed basis in the form of a revision disclosed in the August payrolls report, which found that the economy added fewer than 30k jobs per month over the latest three months – a meaningful slowdown compared with a ~170k monthly average in 2024. While some of this slowdown can be attributed to the public sector (which added no jobs in aggregate over the last three months), many private sector trends also look uninspiring. In particular, BLS data shows the manufacturing sector has shed jobs for four consecutive months and has not added jobs in aggregate over the last three years, leading some observers to conclude that the U.S. remains in a “manufacturing recession”.

THE DIRECTION OF TRAVEL FOR MANY THINGS IN THE THIRD QUARTER WAS “ONWARD AND UPWARD.” NOWHERE WAS THIS CLEARER THAN IN MARKETS, WHERE THE S&P 500 CLOSED AT ALL-TIME HIGHS ON 35% OF ALL TRADING DAYS, THE MOST IN A QUARTER SINCE 1998.

The BLS's Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS), which measures a comprehensive set of labor market fundamentals, continues to exhibit a sluggish environment of "low hires, low fires." JOLTS estimates there are now fewer job vacancies per unemployed person than at any point since 2018 (excluding the early COVID era), indicating it is more difficult for jobless people to get hired. Relatedly, the "quit rate" (percentage of workers voluntarily leaving a job) has fallen below 2% for the first time in nearly ten years (again, excluding the early COVID era), suggesting workers are not finding more attractive employment options elsewhere. While the unemployment rate remains low in a historical context at just 4.3%, economists, including San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, are coalescing around the view that further declines in job vacancies are likely to have pronounced adverse impacts on the unemployment rate, according to the Beveridge Curve in classical macroeconomic theory.

COMPARED WITH THE CONSISTENTLY SLOW AND SOFTENING LABOR MARKET DATA RELEASED DURING THE THIRD QUARTER, MEASURES OF U.S. ECONOMIC OUTPUT HAVE BEEN A BIT MORE ENCOURAGING. AFTER TWO POSITIVE REVISIONS, GDP ACCELERATED TO +3.8% IN THE SECOND QUARTER.

Non-government data similarly reflects weak hiring trends in the U.S. over the last several months. The ADP National Employment Report showed a negative month-over-month change in jobs during June for the first time since 2019. Elsewhere, the Challenger Job Cuts Report from outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas estimates hiring plans in the U.S. are at the lowest level since 2010, while job cuts this year are pacing more than 60% higher year-over-year through August.

Compared with the consistently slow and softening labor market data released during the third quarter, measures of U.S. economic output have been a bit more encouraging. After two positive revisions, GDP accelerated to +3.8% in the second quarter. Additionally, retail sales, as measured by the U.S. Census Bureau, have remained resilient of late, expanding in three consecutive months through the summer for the first time since late 2024. The latest company earnings cycle in the U.S. similarly showed no signs of deterioration, with more than 80% of S&P 500 constituents beating revenue expectations – the best top-line "beat rate" since 2021.

On the cautious side, the aforementioned GDP figure reported for the second quarter may largely reflect tariff-related timing dynamics, as the gross private domestic investment component (private sector spend on new productive assets) fell quarter-over-quarter at the steepest pace in five years. Separately, U.S. "manufacturing recession" dynamics were further evident in the Institute for Supply Management's Purchasing Manager Index, which has measured "contraction" in the sector in 34 of the last 36 months.

Trends on the inflation front have also been inconclusive, though no worse than expected, as Trump's tariffs take effect. June's Consumer Price Index showed some signs of tariff inflation in certain categories like furniture, appliances, and apparel, though a further acceleration in subsequent reports has been limited. The Fed's preferred inflation gauge, the Core Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index, did rise during the third quarter to 2.9% year-over-year as of August from 2.8% as of June. However, a temporary tariff-driven increase in inflation has been expected by Fed officials. The Summary of Economic Projections published by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) at a September meeting projects a median expectation for core PCE inflation to exit the year at 3.1% year-over-year.

Speaking of the Fed, the FOMC voted last month 11-1 in favor of a 25bps reduction in the target Federal Funds Rate to 4.0%-4.25%, with recent Trump-appointee Stephen Miran the lone dissenter, favoring a larger 50bps cut. This near-unanimous decision at the September meeting stands in contrast to an earlier July FOMC meeting, where officials were more divided and the decision to hold the policy rate unchanged was met with a rare "double-dissent" from two Fed Governors. Looking forward, Fed Chair Powell noted in an August speech that the Fed is now faced with a "challenging situation", as still-elevated inflation creates tension against the recent slowdown in labor market fundamentals. As of this writing, the market is pricing in an 85% chance of two further rate cuts this year, while opinions on the rate path in 2026 and beyond diverge widely.

Economic fundamentals across Europe remain sluggish though stable, with recent soft data indicating potential for improvement following a "Framework on an Agreement on Reciprocal, Fair, and Balanced Trade" reached between the U.S. and European Union in July. The Hamburg Commercial Bank's Composite Purchasing Manager Index hit a 16-month high in September, led by strength across Spain, Germany, Ireland, and Italy. France, on the other hand, remains plagued by political instability – after former Prime Minister Bayrou lost a no-confidence vote in Parliament and resigned in early September, his successor resigned after less than four weeks in office, marking the shortest Prime Minister tenure in French history. This instability has spooked bond investors, with French sovereign yields now trading above Greek yields and at all-time "wides" to German yields.

The outlook in Japan has also improved somewhat following the announcement of a "Framework Agreement" for a trade deal with the U.S. in July. Most recently, markets welcomed the election of new Prime Minister Takaichi in early October by the ruling Liberal Democratic Party. Takaichi ran for leadership on a policy platform headlined by fiscal spending increases and easy monetary policy – policies which have been digested by markets as "risk-friendly". This leadership shift will have implications for the Bank of Japan, which has increased the policy rate three times in the current cycle (to 0.50% currently), and which has signaled intent to continue hiking, given ongoing improvements in business sentiment, capex plans, and wage growth.

In China, domestic output remains subdued and consumer and business sentiment remain cautious, given a still-weak property sector and trade uncertainty with the U.S. Loose monetary policy and fiscal stimulus programs have provided consumers and businesses some temporary reprieve, but recent estimates from the World Bank call for a further slowing in GDP growth next year.

Financial Markets

The bull market in U.S. stocks broadened during the third quarter, with the S&P 500 notching 23 all-time high closes (the most for any quarter since 1998). The U.S. dollar stabilized following a double-digit decline in the DXY Index during the first six months of the year, and small caps outperformed large caps, evidenced by the Russell 2000 Index hitting a new all-time high for the first time since 2021. That said, strength was broad-based, with the MSCI All Country World Index, Dow Jones Industrial Average, and Nasdaq Composite also hitting new highs during the quarter.

The S&P 500 posted an 8% total return in 3Q, capping a 34% advance off the post-Liberation Day low set on April 8th. The 34% (and counting) increase in stocks ranks as a top five bull market rally over the last 65 years, and is particularly unique compared with other large stock market runs which have tended to overlap with the end of economic recessions. This time around, investors are excited by the combination of improving soft data (consumer confidence surveys, small business optimism, and CEO confidence indicators all up sharply from 2Q lows), prospects for easier monetary policy, productivity gains from artificial intelligence, and implications of a new U.S. omnibus tax and spending bill which promises to drive accelerating growth in capital spending over the coming quarters.

Outside of equities, the benchmark 10 Year U.S. Treasury yield ended 3Q at 4.15%, -10bps lower vs. the start of the quarter given a backdrop of relatively benign inflation data and policy rate easing. Corporate credit spreads across the quality spectrum remain extremely tight by historical standards and continued to tighten during the quarter.

Artificial Intelligence

At the risk of sounding like a broken record, the scale of investment in AI infrastructure, as well as the pace of technological innovation, continues to surpass expectations. The five largest cloud computing platforms (in order of size: Amazon (AMZN) Web Services, Microsoft (MSFT) Azure, Google (GOOGL) Cloud Platform, Meta Platforms (META), and Oracle (ORCL) Cloud Infrastructure) have communicated capex plans for 2025, which aggregate to ~$380bn. This total has been revised 15% higher over the last quarter, 35% higher since the start of the year, and 75% higher compared with a year ago. This is an investment cycle of unprecedented scale which economists at Evercore ISI estimate contributed more than half of the overall U.S. GDP growth during the first half of the year.

The natural questions in the face of such ambitious investment are: “Is this healthy?” “Are we in a bubble?” Indeed, Google Trends reveal that searches for “AI Bubble” increased more than 10x from the start of the year to a recent peak in August. Searches for “Dotcom Bubble” have shown a similar spike on Google, as investors and observers increasingly draw comparisons between the current environment and the Internet bubble of the late-1990s.

At face value, it is difficult to see close parallels in public stock market valuations today compared with the Dotcom Bubble of the late 1990s. To illustrate this point: the seven largest technology companies in the S&P 500 as of this writing trade on median / mean forward P/E multiples of 32x / 29x, respectively. This compares with median / mean forward P/E multiples of the seven largest S&P 500 technology companies at the end of 1999 of 70x / 84x, respectively. Thus, whether or not the stock market is in a “bubble” will likely depend on the extent to which earnings expectations prove achievable. This, in turn, will depend on the pace at which AI infrastructure can deliver financial returns, and on the utility that AI technology can provide more broadly.

In the third quarter, we saw continued rapid adoption of major Chatbot services, with OpenAI’s ChatGPT disclosing more than 800mm weekly active users last month (up 5x year-over-year) and Perplexity disclosing 300mm search queries per week (up 3x since the start of the year). Elsewhere, Amazon management noted deployment of the one millionth robot across its warehouse footprint and said that 75% of all deliveries are now being assisted by robotics. Microsoft and JPMorgan (JPM) were among many companies to cite significant cost savings being generated by AI, with both sizing $500mm+ annualized cost efficiencies thus far. We also started to hear signs that technological progress is nearing another inflection point, as Meta Platforms CEO Mark Zuckerberg told analysts on an earnings call in July that, “We’ve begun to see glimpses of our AI systems improving themselves. The improvement is slow for now, but undeniable.”

Looking Forward

Elevated valuations combined with uncertain economic fundamentals could manifest in an environment of relatively higher stock market volatility over the coming months, and our portfolio companies will not be immune to these disruptions. However, we have confidence that our emphasis on the world’s highest quality management teams, innovative products and business models, and differentiated products will serve us well.

– John T. Belton, CFA & Howard F. Ward, CFA

NOTES AND IMPORTANT INFORMATION

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses of a Fund before investing.

A Fund's NAV per share will fluctuate with changes in the market value of the Fund's portfolio securities. Stocks are subject to market, economic, and business risks that cause their prices to fluctuate. A Fund's use of leverage may magnify the volatility of net asset value changes versus Funds that do not employ leverage. When Fund shares are sold, they may be worth more or less than their original cost. Consequently, you can lose money by investing in a Fund.

data.title: Sticky Japan Inflation And Upbeat Activity Support Rate Hike
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

Japanese inflation remained well above 2%, while stronger-than-expected production and retail sales are likely to reinforce the Bank of Japan’s confidence to hike interest rates in December.

Inflation remained at elevated levels in November

Tokyo's CPI inflation in November was steady, with headline inflation up 2.7% year-on-year and core inflation up 2.8%, largely in line with market expectations. Utilities rose 2.4% as government subsidies ended. Service prices, an important gauge of demand-driven inflation, rose modestly by 1.5%.

In the monthly comparison, it rose 0.3% month on month, seasonally adjusted, with goods and services up by 0.5% and 0.2%, respectively. Although growth slowed compared to the 0.6% gain seen in October, the data still indicated persistent upward pressure.

Going forward, we expect headline inflation to decline, likely to below 2%. Rice prices, which had been rising 90% in early summer, decelerated to 40% in November.

We expect further deceleration in the coming months. Also, the government’s new economic package will focus on lowering energy prices. This will likely lower headline inflation. Still, we believe core inflation is likely to remain around current levels, driven by a weak JPY and strong wage growth.

Inflation rose well above 2% in November

Monthly activity data beat market consensus, showing a clear recovery sign of the economy

We believe manufacturing activity has normalised once a trade agreement was officially reached between the US and Japan. It seems like the auto sector is benefiting the most. Industrial production extended its solid gain for a second month, unexpectedly rising 1.4% MoM in October (vs. 2.6% in September, -0.6% market consensus).

The most notable gains were from cars and petrochemicals. Car output rose 7.2% in October, recording three consecutive monthly rises. Petrochemicals also extended their gains for the second month.

Retail sales increased by 1.6% MoM, compared to a revised flat reading in September and the market consensus of 0.8%. Growth was broad-based across major categories, with auto sales posting the largest increase at 10.9%.

GDP and BoJ outlook

Today's data indicate that the economy is recovering following a temporary contraction in the third quarter of 2025. The robust performance of the automotive sector was particularly noteworthy.

We anticipate GDP growth of 0.2% quarter-on-quarter in the fourth quarter of 2025. The current data support our projection, but introduce some potential upside risks.

The Bank of Japan has expressed concern that US tariffs might negatively impact the economy. However, today's data is expected to boost the BoJ's confidence in Japan's economic growth. Given steady inflation and the ongoing recovery of the economy, we believe the likelihood of a rate hike on 19 December has increased.

data.title: Health Care's Epic Rally
data.description:
As the Tech/AI trade has hit a speed bump this month, the Health Care sector has been shot out of a cannon. As of Wednesday's close, the S&P 500 Health Care sector was 10% above its 50-day moving average and 2.5 standard deviations above its 50 DMA. Check out the one-year trading range chart of the sector below:

Health Care's move in the last month has left a good chunk of the stocks in the sector at new 52-week highs. Net new 52-week highs for the sector have exploded to the highest level of the last year:

So far this month, the Health Care sector is outperforming the broad S&P 500 by more than ten percentage points. As shown below, that hasn't happened since November 2000!

While Health Care is catching a bid these days, it's still deep in the hole based on its actions over the last couple of years. Just over two years ago, Health Care's weighting in the S&P 500 was at 16%. By this September, its weighting had nearly been cut in half, down to just 8.65%, its lowest weighting since August 1994.

Health Care's 18% gain in the last two months has only pushed its weight in the S&P back up to 10% as of Thursday, so there's plenty of runway left for the sector if the good vibes continue.

Below is a look at the most extreme overbought stocks in the sector right now. Even names like Bristol Myers (BMY) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) have joined in on the rally.

Of course, after moves like this, most traders would tell you to wait for some downside mean reversion before jumping in on the long side.

data.title: ClearBridge Large Cap Growth Portfolios Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Equities surged in the third quarter, fueled by better-than-feared tariff outcomes, the passing of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act in July, anticipated interest rate cuts — the Federal Reserve cut 25 bps cut in September and signaled further easing — and robust corporate earnings, particularly in technology and the Magnificent Seven. The benchmark Russell 1000 Growth Index rose 10.5% for the quarter and is up 46.7% from its early April lows.
  • Contributors: Holdings in Microsoft (MSFT), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), ASML (ASML) and not holding Costco Wholesale (COST). Stock selection in consumer staples.
  • Detractors: Holdings in Netflix (NFLX), Intuit (INTU) and Intuitive Surgical (ISRG), underweights to Apple (AAPL) and Alphabet (GOOGL). Stock selection in information technology (IT), communication services and health care.
  • Outlook: Looking ahead to 2026, we are optimistic but also cautious. While AI winners continue to dominate market attention, the Portfolios are prepared for a range of scenarios, including stagflation or a rate-cutting rally.

Performance Review

  • A significant theme since Liberation Day has been exuberance around AI and divergence between “AI winners” and “AI losers.” AI winners, consisting primarily of cloud providers, chip makers and certain infrastructure software companies, have rallied even more strongly from the April lows while capital has flowed out of perceived AI laggards in areas like application software and services.
  • The Portfolios have underperformed the benchmark due to underexposure to perceived AI winners (Broadcom (AVGO), Oracle (ORCL), not owning Palantir Technologies (PLTR)) as well as holding several names deemed by investors to be AI losers (Workday (WDAY), Salesforce (CRM), Accenture (ACN)). Several of our AI-indexed holdings were leading contributors in the quarter including cloud hyperscaler Microsoft, contract chip manufacturer Taiwan Semiconductor and chip equipment maker ASML.
  • Netflix delivered strong second quarter results but was a detractor as the video streamer reported that operating margins would weaken in the second half of 2025 due to increased spending on content and marketing. Intuit also reported strong quarterly results but was pressured by full year guidance that didn’t surprise to the upside.
  • The health care sector is facing a myriad of questions around underlying spending levels, tariff concerns and regulatory risks. Medical robotics maker Intuitive Surgical was lower in the quarter due to those headwinds and higher costs leading to pressure on margins.
  • The Portfolios initiated new positions in infrastructure software providers Oracle and Datadog (DDOG) and added to custom silicon developer Broadcom. Oracle, a leading provider of database software for large enterprises, has successfully expanded into cloud infrastructure as a platform to run generative AI workloads. Datadog operates a monitoring, observability and data security platform for cloud applications, a large and growing end market with penetration rising as use and complexity of applications grow.
  • We exited positions in Workday and Accenture, lowering our exposure to application software providers given decelerating fundamental growth and growing risks around AI.
  • In health care, we exited managed care provider UnitedHealth Group (UNH) following remarks from returning CEO Stephen Helmsley about a turnaround plan that indicated the path to recovery will be protracted. Ongoing uncertainty impacting pharmaceutical companies, as well as our more muted view of the GLP-1 market, prompted the sale of leading diabetes and obesity franchises Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO). We upgraded our biopharmaceutical exposure with the purchase of Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), a high-quality biotech with a leading franchise in cystic fibrosis.
  • We see promise in early cyclical companies that could be beneficiaries of an improving economic environment. We repurchased Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG), which is coming off a period of relatively weaker sales we view as more macro than execution related, increased our Starbucks (SBUX) position.

Outlook

  • Momentum remains strong and valuations high across the large cap market, most notably among growth stocks. We have purposely positioned the Portfolios as a strategy that seeks to deliver consistent results through the business cycle. When the Russell 1000 Growth Index compounds at its historical average of 11.9%, the Portfolios have shown an ability to outperform. We also have a track record of strong downside capture during challenged market environments as demonstrated in the first quarter of this year. Due to our focus on diversification and risk management, however, we tend to lag the benchmark in higher-return, beta-driven momentum periods like the current one.
  • Despite the near-term performance challenges, nothing has changed in our investment approach or management of the portfolio. We have always tried to buy names at attractive entry points, using our long-term orientation to take advantage of periods of volatility like the aftermath of Liberation Day. We continue to lean on our three growth buckets framework with the core of the portfolio in reasonably priced stable growth companies that compound earnings over time, supported by higher growth select names gaining share through innovation and cyclical growers with turnaround potential or direct exposure to economic activity.
  • Looking ahead to 2026, we are optimistic but also cautious. While AI winners continue to dominate market attention, the Portfolios are prepared for a range of scenarios, including stagflation or a rate-cutting rally.
